directions.name: Retry PTX Hot-Band Grouping With A 4-Row Window
directions.hypothesis: Round 10/17 closed the non-PTX 128x128 sibling decisively: it preserved correctness but blew DRAM up to 34.17 and regressed runtime to 26.70438385 ms, so the path away from the PTX microkernel is not competitive. That pushes the search back to the surviving PTX-adjacent levers on top of the accepted export base. The prior grouped-row retune to 5 was measured before the new export cleanup became the accepted baseline. Reopening the grouping family with a 4-row window is the next best bounded move because it keeps the proven PTX/export path intact while retesting CTA orchestration with a more symmetric grouping size than 5.
directions.expected_bottleneck: CTA grouping and orchestration overhead around the PTX hot-band grouped-row mapping.
directions.risk: Medium. The family already posted one losing attempt at 5 rows, but the accepted base has changed since then and a 4-row window is closer to the old grouped baseline than the rejected 5-row retune.
directions.stop_condition: Stop this reopened grouping family if the 4-row window still fails to beat 24.84582424 ms or simply recreates the old grouping loss pattern.

directions.name: Reopen The Measured 64x384 Fixed-Main-Tile Control Path
directions.hypothesis: With the non-PTX 128x128 sibling now closed and the reopened prefetch family also negative, the older `64x384` fixed-main-tile sweep remains the main broader fallback with direct measured ancestry. It still trails the grouping retry because the evidence is older and the branch is much wider, but it is the best non-PTX control left in the queue.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Broader hot-band path selection and arithmetic-intensity tradeoff rather than PTX helper overhead.
directions.risk: Medium. The family has explicit sweep evidence, but it is a broad branch change that can easily give back the wins from the accepted PTX/export base.
directions.stop_condition: Stop immediately if the 64x384 control path remains materially slower than the accepted PTX base or reopens a broad-family regression.

directions.name: Freeze PTX Launch And Recheck Only The Peeled 384 Tail Split
directions.hypothesis: If both the grouping retry and the 64x384 control fail, the last bounded family still inside the current fixed-shape launch path is the seam between the PTX hot-band kernel and the peeled 384-row band. A narrow control at that seam could expose whether the current accepted PTX microkernel is fine and the remaining cost is actually in the follow-on peeled region or the hot-band / peeled handoff.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Boundary and launch split cost between the PTX hot-band kernel and the peeled 384-row row-band path.
directions.risk: Medium. This stays in the default fixed-shape launch path, but it is less directly supported by prior measurements than the first two directions.
directions.stop_condition: Only revisit this seam if the higher-ranked directions stall, and stop immediately if it broadens beyond a narrow handoff change.

notes: Round 11/17 diagnosis for run 20260420_162632_bf16_gemm_v1_948fc5c. Human-review mapping for this round: keep the deeper export cleanup, reopened prefetch-handoff, and non-PTX 128x128 control all closed after their measured losses. The new evidence is especially clear for the non-PTX control: correctness stayed intact, but DRAM jumped to 34.17 and runtime regressed to 26.70438385 ms, so moving off the PTX microkernel is not the next win. No new explicit human idea family is queued in state/human_review.md, so the ranking now returns to the remaining bounded PTX-adjacent and launch-seam families: accept a grouping retry with a 4-row window on top of the accepted export base, defer the older 64x384 control as the broader fallback, and keep the hot-band / peeled seam only as a tertiary option.
